meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a GSAP architecture decision is right or wrong. Production-grade logic scaffolds.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Use a timeline when motion has multiple dependent beats.
logic: Independent tweens hide sequence logic.
Timelines make timing reviewable.
Labels let designers and engineers discuss beats precisely.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Create named beats such as intro, reveal, settle, exit.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Every GSAP animation in a framework component must have a documented cleanup strategy. Without cleanup, the animation is broken in production.
logic: Modern SPAs navigate without full page reloads. Components mount and unmount frequently.
A GSAP tween holds a reference to a DOM node. When the node unmounts but the tween persists, the tween continues to calculate values on garbage-collected targets.
On re-mount, a new tween is created alongside the old one, now animating the same element twice with conflicting values.
After 3-4 navigations, the page accumulates N overlapping animations per element. CPU usage climbs. Interactions become unresponsive.
Cleanup is not a 'nice to have' — without it, the animation system degrades on every navigation cycle.
reasoning_chains.so_what: In React: use gsap.context() with ctx.revert() in useEffect cleanup. In Vue: kill animations in onBeforeUnmount. In Svelte: kill in onDestroy. In vanilla JS: track animation references and call .kill() before creating new ones. Add a lint rule that flags useEffect without animation cleanup when GSAP is imported.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: ScrollTrigger density above ~10 instances per page reliably degrades scroll performance, not animation quality.
logic: Each ScrollTrigger registers a scroll listener and calculates trigger positions on every scroll event.
Scroll position calculation involves getBoundingClientRect() — a layout-triggering call that forces synchronous layout when called inside a scroll handler.
With 20+ triggers, each scroll event triggers 20+ layout recalculations. At 120Hz scroll wheels, this saturates the main thread.
The visual result — stutter — undermines the premium feel that scroll animation was meant to create.
GSAP recommends batching triggers: fewer triggers, each driving more elements.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Audit ScrollTrigger count. If above 10, batch: group adjacent elements under a single trigger with a shared timeline. Use ScrollTrigger.refresh() after images load and layout settles. For content-heavy pages, prefer IntersectionObserver for simple reveals and reserve ScrollTrigger for scrub-driven storytelling where scroll position directly controls animation progress.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: The position parameter is the most underused GSAP feature. It turns timelines from a sequence into a choreography language.
logic: Without position parameters, timelines are linear: each tween starts after the previous one ends.
With position parameters, timelines express musical relationships: 'this tween starts 0.2s after that label,' 'these two tweens overlap by 0.1s,' 'this group starts together.'
Overlapping tweens create rhythm and organic feel. Pure sequential tweens read as mechanical.
Labels combined with position parameters create a choreography grammar that is readable by both humans and the engine.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Use labels for every semantic beat. Use relative positioning: `'-=0.1'` for tight coupling, `'+=0.3'` for breathing room, `'label'` for sync points. Never use absolute seconds as delays — they break when the sequence length changes. Teach the team to read a timeline as a score, not a script.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: GSAP is one of the most performant animation libraries available. When GSAP animation stutters, the cause is almost never GSAP — it's the animated properties or the DOM structure.
logic: GSAP updates values and applies them via requestAnimationFrame. The overhead is negligible.
Performance drops come from browser rendering phases triggered by the animated properties: layout (width, height, top, left), paint (color, background, box-shadow), or composite (transform, opacity).
An agent's instinct — 'GSAP is slow, switch to CSS' — is wrong. CSS animations on the same layout-triggering properties will be equally slow.
The fix is not changing the library. The fix is changing the animated properties to compositor-only equivalents.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before optimizing GSAP code, profile with DevTools Performance panel. Identify whether the bottleneck is in script (unlikely), layout (likely if animating width/height/margin), paint (likely if animating color/shadow), or composite. Switch layout-triggering properties to transform equivalents. Switch paint-triggering properties to opacity or composite-friendly alternatives. Then re-profile to confirm.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Plugins solve specific animation problems. Using a plugin when a simpler approach works adds complexity, bundle size, and mental overhead without benefit.
logic: GSAP plugins (ScrollTrigger, Flip, SplitText, MotionPath, etc.) are powerful but specialized.

SplitText is for character/word animation. If you're splitting only to apply a simple fade, CSS `overflow: hidden` + a single transform tween on a parent achieves the same result with zero plugin cost.
Flip is for layout state transitions. If you're animating a known from/to state that can be expressed as two tweens, Flip adds an unnecessary layout snapshot step.
The plugin decision ladder: can this be solved with a core tween? → timeline? → CSS transition? → then consider a plugin.
reasoning_chains.so_what: For every GSAP animation task, ask: 'Can this be done with gsap.to() and transform/opacity?' If yes, do that. Plugins are for the cases where core GSAP genuinely can't express the intent — not for cases where a plugin makes the code 10% shorter.
